purpose: Groups each extracted fact from the 2026-07-07 batch with its chapter/topic/source-file context and a recommendation for how (or whether) it should eventually be used, so a reviewer can triage the batch without re-reading every source photo. Nothing here is verified -- needsVerification is true on every entry per the source intake's verificationStatus, which stays 'unverified' until a fact is checked directly against Vinci's confirmed manual. This file does not create or modify any question in course.json.

recommendedUseGuide.learn-mode-note: Conceptual/framing content better suited to Learn Mode's progressive-disclosure explanations than a discrete testable question.
recommendedUseGuide.quiz-question: Specific, testable, text-answerable fact -- a reasonable candidate for a future quiz/drill question once verified.
recommendedUseGuide.visual-question: Fact is best tested by showing the source image (diagram, photo, table, or symbol) alongside the question, per the app's optional image/imageAlt question fields.
recommendedUseGuide.instructor-evidence: Useful to show what material has been covered and reviewed, but not yet solid enough (photo quality, cross-reference, or paraphrase-vs-verbatim concerns) to turn directly into a quoted question.
recommendedUseGuide.do-not-use: Flagged as unsuitable for question-writing -- e.g. a redundant duplicate of another source file's fact, or a specific wording/spelling risk.

facts.topic: IMO/LSA safety symbol pictograms (lifeboat, rescue boat, liferaft, lifebuoy, lifejacket, EPIRB, immersion suit, exit signs, etc.)
facts.fact: Green pictograms mark life-saving equipment/locations (Lifeboat, Rescue Boat, Liferaft, Davit Launched Liferaft, Embarkation Ladder, Pilot Ladder).
facts.confusionNote: Easily confused with other equipment-location pictograms of similar shape/color.

facts.topic: IMO/LSA safety symbol pictograms (lifeboat, rescue boat, liferaft, lifebuoy, lifejacket, EPIRB, immersion suit, exit signs, etc.)
facts.fact: Personal flotation symbols include Lifebuoy, Lifebuoy with Line, Lifebuoy with Light, Lifebuoy with Light and Smoke, Lifejacket, and Child Lifejacket as visually distinct pictograms.
facts.confusionNote: 'Lifebuoy with Light' vs 'Lifebuoy with Light and Smoke' are visually similar, differing only in the smoke marker.

facts.topic: IMO/LSA safety symbol pictograms (lifeboat, rescue boat, liferaft, lifebuoy, lifejacket, EPIRB, immersion suit, exit signs, etc.)
facts.fact: Signaling/rescue equipment symbols include EPIRB, Radar Transponder, Survival Craft Portable Radio, Survival Craft Pyrotechnics, Rocket Parachute Flares, and Line-Throwing Appliance.

facts.topic: IMO/LSA safety symbol pictograms (lifeboat, rescue boat, liferaft, lifebuoy, lifejacket, EPIRB, immersion suit, exit signs, etc.)
facts.fact: Medical/assembly symbols include Assembly Station, Stretcher, Medical Locker, TPA (Thermal Protective Aid), and AES (Anti-Exposure Suit).
facts.confusionNote: AES (Anti-Exposure Suit) vs Immersion Suit vs TPA (Thermal Protective Aid) are three distinct thermal-protection symbols easily conflated.

facts.topic: IMO/LSA safety symbol pictograms (lifeboat, rescue boat, liferaft, lifebuoy, lifejacket, EPIRB, immersion suit, exit signs, etc.)
facts.fact: Blue/purple pictograms mark operating instructions rather than equipment locations: Fasten Seat Belts, Secure Hatches, Start Engine, Lower Lifeboat, Lower Liferaft, Lower Rescue Boat, Release Falls, Start Water Supply, Start Air Supply, Release Gripes.
facts.confusionNote: Blue/purple 'operating instruction' symbols can be visually confused with green 'equipment/location' symbols.

facts.topic: IMO/LSA safety symbol pictograms (lifeboat, rescue boat, liferaft, lifebuoy, lifejacket, EPIRB, immersion suit, exit signs, etc.)
facts.fact: Exit/escape symbols include Stairs to Fire Exit, a directional arrow, Exit (running figure + arrow), Escape Hatch, and Escape Route.

facts.topic: C.O.I (Certificate of Inspection) — sample document and lifeboat-capacity / certified-lifeboatmen table
facts.fact: Every US-Flagged ship has a C.O.I. (Certificate of Inspection) issued by the USCG.
facts.confusionNote: Vessel-level C.O.I. (USCG-issued) can be confused with a lifeboat's own nameplate/capacity markings, which is a separate boat-level document covered elsewhere in this batch.

facts.topic: C.O.I (Certificate of Inspection) — sample document and lifeboat-capacity / certified-lifeboatmen table
facts.fact: The C.O.I. states the minimum number of Endorsed Lifeboatmen required aboard.

facts.topic: C.O.I (Certificate of Inspection) — sample document and lifeboat-capacity / certified-lifeboatmen table
facts.fact: Lifeboat capacity 1-25 requires 1 certified lifeboatman.
facts.confusionNote: Table boundary values (e.g. 25 vs 26, 40 vs 41) are easy to get off-by-one on.

facts.topic: C.O.I (Certificate of Inspection) — sample document and lifeboat-capacity / certified-lifeboatmen table
facts.fact: Lifeboat capacity 26-40 requires 2 certified lifeboatmen.
facts.confusionNote: Table boundary values (e.g. 25 vs 26, 40 vs 41) are easy to get off-by-one on.

facts.topic: C.O.I (Certificate of Inspection) — sample document and lifeboat-capacity / certified-lifeboatmen table
facts.fact: Lifeboat capacity 41-60 requires 3 certified lifeboatmen.
facts.confusionNote: Table boundary values (e.g. 60 vs 61) are easy to get off-by-one on.

facts.topic: C.O.I (Certificate of Inspection) — sample document and lifeboat-capacity / certified-lifeboatmen table
facts.fact: Lifeboat capacity 61-85 requires 4 certified lifeboatmen.
facts.confusionNote: Table boundary values (e.g. 85 vs 86) are easy to get off-by-one on.

facts.topic: C.O.I (Certificate of Inspection) — sample document and lifeboat-capacity / certified-lifeboatmen table
facts.fact: Lifeboat capacity 86-110 requires 5 certified lifeboatmen.
facts.confusionNote: Table boundary values (e.g. 110) are easy to get off-by-one on.

facts.topic: C.O.I (Certificate of Inspection) — sample document and lifeboat-capacity / certified-lifeboatmen table
facts.fact: Lifeboat capacity 110 and above requires 6 certified lifeboatmen.
facts.confusionNote: Table boundary values (e.g. 110) are easy to get off-by-one on.

facts.topic: Lifeboat types — Partially Enclosed and Totally Enclosed (roller gravity davit)
facts.fact: Five lifeboat types: open, partially enclosed, totally enclosed, totally enclosed with self-contained air support, totally enclosed fire protected.

facts.topic: Lifeboat types — Partially Enclosed and Totally Enclosed (roller gravity davit)
facts.fact: Lifeboats are not approved to carry more than 150 people.

facts.topic: Lifeboat types — Partially Enclosed and Totally Enclosed (roller gravity davit)
facts.fact: Passenger ships require boarding of all people in 10 minutes or less; cargo ships require at least twice as many seats as crew, boardable in 3 minutes or less.
facts.confusionNote: The 10-minute (passenger ship) and 3-minute (cargo ship) boarding figures are easy to swap.

facts.topic: Lifeboat types — Partially Enclosed and Totally Enclosed (roller gravity davit)
facts.fact: The maximum number of people, ship's name, and lifeboat number must be clearly marked on the side of the lifeboat.

facts.topic: Lifeboat types — Partially Enclosed and Totally Enclosed (roller gravity davit)
facts.fact: Non-self-righting lifeboats must have handholds on the bottom of the boat.
facts.confusionNote: Assumes all lifeboats self-right; only some do, and non-self-righting types compensate with bottom handholds.

facts.topic: Lifeboat types — Partially Enclosed and Totally Enclosed (roller gravity davit)
facts.fact: Partially enclosed lifeboats have permanent rigid covers on at least 20% of both sides, plus a foldable canopy that can fully enclose the boat.
facts.confusionNote: Partially enclosed vs totally enclosed lifeboats are easy to conflate as differing only in degree.

facts.topic: Lifeboat types — Partially Enclosed and Totally Enclosed (roller gravity davit)
facts.fact: Totally enclosed lifeboats have a rigid watertight cover, boarding hatches, windows, and seat safety belts.

facts.topic: Lifeboat/rescue boat types continued — Totally Enclosed (free-fall davit), Fast Rescue Boat (FRB)
facts.fact: Totally enclosed lifeboats are self-righting — if capsized, they automatically flip back upright, and ventilators automatically close during capsize.

facts.topic: Lifeboat/rescue boat types continued — Totally Enclosed (free-fall davit), Fast Rescue Boat (FRB)
facts.fact: Self-contained air support systems: if hatches/ventilation are properly secured, the air supply lasts ten minutes, with slight positive pressure keeping out fumes/gases (air not recirculated).
facts.confusionNote: The 10-minute (air-support duration) and 8-minute (fire-protected water-spray duration) figures appear in the same paragraph and are easy to swap.

facts.topic: Lifeboat/rescue boat types continued — Totally Enclosed (free-fall davit), Fast Rescue Boat (FRB)
facts.fact: Totally enclosed fire-protected lifeboats have a water spray system protecting occupants from an oil fire in the water for at least 8 minutes.
facts.confusionNote: The 10-minute (air-support duration) and 8-minute (fire-protected water-spray duration) figures appear in the same paragraph and are easy to swap.

facts.topic: Lifeboat/rescue boat types continued — Totally Enclosed (free-fall davit), Fast Rescue Boat (FRB)
facts.fact: Rescue boats must carry at least 5 seated passengers plus a person on a stretcher, all wearing immersion suits and/or life jackets.

facts.topic: Lifeboat/rescue boat types continued — Totally Enclosed (free-fall davit), Fast Rescue Boat (FRB)
facts.fact: Rescue boats must maneuver at 6 knots for 4 hours, and have a permanently fitted towing arrangement capable of towing the ship's largest fully-loaded lifeboat at 2 knots.
facts.confusionNote: Rescue boat's 6-knot cruising speed vs its 2-knot towing speed are easy to swap.

facts.topic: Lifeboat/rescue boat types continued — Totally Enclosed (free-fall davit), Fast Rescue Boat (FRB)
facts.fact: Rescue boats with outboard motors must have fuel tanks protected against fire.

facts.topic: Markings — required vessel name/port/persons/boat-number markings, nameplate contents
facts.fact: Each lifeboat/rescue boat bow must be marked (both sides) in block capital letters/numbers with the vessel's name and the home port (port name marked on the vessel's stern).
facts.confusionNote: What's marked on the BOW (vessel name + port, block capitals) vs. what's on the NAMEPLATE (manufacturer, weights, persons) is easy to conflate.

facts.topic: Markings — required vessel name/port/persons/boat-number markings, nameplate contents
facts.fact: The number of persons the boat is equipped for must be marked, preferably on the bow, in permanent characters, and must not exceed the number shown on the nameplate.
facts.confusionNote: The 'number of persons' marking must not exceed the nameplate figure -- students may assume it just needs to match built capacity.

facts.topic: Markings — required vessel name/port/persons/boat-number markings, nameplate contents
facts.fact: The lifeboat number and vessel identification must be marked so it's visible from above the boat.

facts.topic: Markings — required vessel name/port/persons/boat-number markings, nameplate contents
facts.fact: Each lifeboat's nameplate/label must list: manufacturer's name & serial number/date of manufacture, light load and full load condition/weights, and the number of persons allowed.

facts.topic: Markings — required vessel name/port/persons/boat-number markings, nameplate contents
facts.fact: The vessel's name must be clearly printed on every oar, every life preserver (PFD), each life ring, and the EPIRB & SART.
facts.confusionNote: Source manual literally prints "EPRIB" (likely a typo for EPIRB, used correctly elsewhere in this batch); confirm exact spelling before quoting verbatim in a question.

facts.topic: Markings — required vessel name/port/persons/boat-number markings, nameplate contents
facts.fact: 'Open Lifeboat' is described as an older style being phased out; 'Lifeboat Tender' is common on passenger ships, serving as both a tender and a lifeboat when necessary.

facts.topic: Coloring — international orange / Indian orange / red release-lever color requirements
facts.fact: Canopy exteriors of enclosed lifeboats are painted international orange.

facts.topic: Coloring — international orange / Indian orange / red release-lever color requirements
facts.fact: Tops of thwarts, side benches, and footings of open lifeboats are painted international orange.

facts.topic: Coloring — international orange / Indian orange / red release-lever color requirements
facts.fact: Sails and canvas canopies must be dyed Indian orange (a distinct term from international orange).
facts.confusionNote: "International orange" and "Indian orange" are distinct terms for different components and easy to conflate.

facts.topic: Coloring — international orange / Indian orange / red release-lever color requirements
facts.fact: The release lever must be red.
facts.confusionNote: Students may assume the area around the release lever is also orange, when it must be a contrasting light color.

facts.topic: Coloring — international orange / Indian orange / red release-lever color requirements
facts.fact: The area immediately surrounding the release-lever control must be a sharply contrasting light color.
facts.confusionNote: Students may assume the area around the release lever is also orange, when it must be a contrasting light color.

facts.topic: Coloring — international orange / Indian orange / red release-lever color requirements
facts.fact: Canopy exteriors of enclosed lifeboats are painted international orange.
facts.confusionNote: Duplicate content -- this source file is a near-duplicate photo of the same page as vinci-2026-07-07-041663-1. Use that source file's facts as the canonical version; do not generate a second, redundant question from this duplicate.

facts.topic: Coloring — international orange / Indian orange / red release-lever color requirements
facts.fact: Tops of thwarts, side benches, and footings of open lifeboats are painted international orange.
facts.confusionNote: Duplicate content -- see vinci-2026-07-07-041663-1.

facts.topic: Coloring — international orange / Indian orange / red release-lever color requirements
facts.fact: Sails and canvas canopies must be dyed Indian orange (a distinct term from international orange).
facts.confusionNote: Duplicate content -- see vinci-2026-07-07-041663-1.

facts.topic: Coloring — international orange / Indian orange / red release-lever color requirements
facts.fact: The release lever must be red.
facts.confusionNote: Duplicate content -- see vinci-2026-07-07-041663-1.

facts.topic: Coloring — international orange / Indian orange / red release-lever color requirements
facts.fact: The area immediately surrounding the release-lever control must be a sharply contrasting light color.
facts.confusionNote: Duplicate content -- see vinci-2026-07-07-041663-1.

facts.topic: Numbering (odd/starboard, even/port lifeboat numbering) and Capacity (150-person max, seating/mass rules)
facts.fact: Odd-numbered lifeboats are on the starboard side; even-numbered lifeboats are on the port side.
facts.confusionNote: Odd/starboard vs even/port is a classic swap risk (mnemonic can be inverted).

facts.topic: Numbering (odd/starboard, even/port lifeboat numbering) and Capacity (150-person max, seating/mass rules)
facts.fact: Both odd and even numbering run from forward (FWD) to aft.

facts.topic: Numbering (odd/starboard, even/port lifeboat numbering) and Capacity (150-person max, seating/mass rules)
facts.fact: Lifeboat numbers must be marked on each side of the bow in block capital letters.

facts.topic: Numbering (odd/starboard, even/port lifeboat numbering) and Capacity (150-person max, seating/mass rules)
facts.fact: No lifeboat may be approved to accommodate more than 150 persons.

facts.topic: Numbering (odd/starboard, even/port lifeboat numbering) and Capacity (150-person max, seating/mass rules)
facts.fact: Capacity is the LESSER of: (1) persons at average mass 165 lbs (passenger ship) or 188 lbs (cargo ship), wearing lifejackets, seated normally without interfering with propulsion/equipment; or (2) number of seating-arrangement spaces, which may overlap if footrests are fitted and vertical separation between upper/lower seat is at least 350 mm.
facts.confusionNote: The 165 lbs (passenger ship) vs 188 lbs (cargo ship) average-mass figures are easy to swap; students may also assume the greater of the two capacity calculations is used rather than the lesser.

facts.topic: Numbering (odd/starboard, even/port lifeboat numbering) and Capacity (150-person max, seating/mass rules)
facts.fact: Example: lifeboat 2 is forward of lifeboat 4 (port side); lifeboat 7 is abaft (aft of) lifeboat 5 (starboard side).

facts.topic: Design, Construction, and Performance of Modern Lifeboats — 46CFR160.135-7 / IMO LSA Code spec table
facts.fact: Applies to lifeboats built 1998+, per 46CFR160.135-7 / IMO LSA Code.

facts.topic: Design, Construction, and Performance of Modern Lifeboats — 46CFR160.135-7 / IMO LSA Code spec table
facts.fact: All lifeboats except totally enclosed ones must be operable by someone wearing an immersion suit.
facts.confusionNote: Students may assume ALL lifeboats must be operable by someone in an immersion suit -- totally enclosed lifeboats are the explicit exception.

facts.topic: Design, Construction, and Performance of Modern Lifeboats — 46CFR160.135-7 / IMO LSA Code spec table
facts.fact: Operator's station must give 360-degree visibility when seated (moving head/torso to see around obstructions).

facts.topic: Design, Construction, and Performance of Modern Lifeboats — 46CFR160.135-7 / IMO LSA Code spec table
facts.fact: Rigid structural components: steel, aluminum, FRP, or Commandant-approved equivalent.

facts.topic: Design, Construction, and Performance of Modern Lifeboats — 46CFR160.135-7 / IMO LSA Code spec table
facts.fact: Structural strength: 5-knot launch, 3 m (10 ft) drop at full weight, hold 2x allowable weight suspended from falls, 3.5 m/s lateral impact — all without deformation.

facts.topic: Design, Construction, and Performance of Modern Lifeboats — 46CFR160.135-7 / IMO LSA Code spec table
facts.fact: Capacity not to exceed 150 persons, calculated at average mass 75 kg (165 lbs); seats must support 100 kg (220 lbs) each.
facts.confusionNote: The average-mass capacity figure (75 kg/165 lbs) is easy to confuse with the per-seat structural load rating (100 kg/220 lbs).

facts.topic: Design, Construction, and Performance of Modern Lifeboats — 46CFR160.135-7 / IMO LSA Code spec table
facts.fact: Boarding time for full complement: passenger ship 10 min, cargo ship 3 min.
facts.confusionNote: The 10-minute (passenger ship) and 3-minute (cargo ship) boarding figures are easy to swap.

facts.topic: Design, Construction, and Performance of Modern Lifeboats — 46CFR160.135-7 / IMO LSA Code spec table
facts.fact: Engine: compression ignition, EPA-compliant, starts under 2 min at -15C (5F), makes 6 knots (2 knots towing a 25-person liferaft) for 24 hrs fully loaded, shrouded propeller, posted operating instructions.
facts.confusionNote: The engine's normal 6-knot speed vs its 2-knot towing speed are easy to swap.

facts.topic: Spec table continued — Steering, Handholds, Release Mechanism, Skates, Painter Release, Lighting
facts.fact: Steering: rudder must be permanently attached and have a tiller as backup steering (in addition to a wheel).
facts.confusionNote: Page is cropped on the right edge; treat as instructor background evidence, not a directly quotable fact, until re-photographed.

facts.topic: Spec table continued — Steering, Handholds, Release Mechanism, Skates, Painter Release, Lighting
facts.fact: Handholds must be provided on topsides of the lifeboat and/or via buoyant lifelines; non-self-righting lifeboats must additionally have hand-holds (grab rails) on the underside.
facts.confusionNote: Handholds on topsides/lifelines (general requirement) vs hand-holds on underside (non-self-righting only) is easy to conflate. Page is cropped; needs a clearer photo before quiz use.

facts.topic: Spec table continued — Steering, Handholds, Release Mechanism, Skates, Painter Release, Lighting
facts.fact: Release Mechanism: the release lever/control must be red, with a sharply contrasting light color immediately surrounding it.
facts.confusionNote: This exact fact (red lever, contrasting surround) is independently confirmed at high confidence on the 'Coloring' page in this same batch -- prefer that source file for quiz use.

facts.topic: Spec table continued — Steering, Handholds, Release Mechanism, Skates, Painter Release, Lighting
facts.fact: An illustrated operating instruction plate/placard (off-load and emergency on-load release procedure, plus recovery procedure) must be posted, visible/legible from the coxswain's operating position, corrosion-resistant, weatherproof, and marked with the word "Danger".
facts.confusionNote: Students may assume the placard only needs to show the release procedure, missing the recovery procedure and 'Danger' marking requirement. Page is cropped; needs a clearer photo before quiz use.

facts.topic: Spec table continued — Steering, Handholds, Release Mechanism, Skates, Painter Release, Lighting
facts.fact: Skates: lifeboats launched down the ship's side must have skates and fenders to prevent launch damage.
facts.confusionNote: Page is cropped; needs a clearer photo before quiz use.

facts.topic: Spec table continued — Steering, Handholds, Release Mechanism, Skates, Painter Release, Lighting
facts.fact: Lighting: a manually controlled lamp must be fitted both inside and outside, run continuously for 12 hours; oil lamps are not permitted for this purpose.
facts.confusionNote: Oil lamps are explicitly disallowed for the 12-hour lighting requirement -- easy to miss. Page is cropped; needs a clearer photo before quiz use.

facts.topic: Construction / Lifeboat Shell — hull terminology (gunwale, stem, keel, stern post)
facts.fact: Lifeboats are the primary lifesaving equipment onboard, designed to keep people alive long enough to be rescued — not to get them home.

facts.topic: Construction / Lifeboat Shell — hull terminology (gunwale, stem, keel, stern post)
facts.fact: Open lifeboats are normally double-ended (pointed at both ends), making them more seaworthy (less likely to be swamped or to 'broach to').
facts.confusionNote: "Double-ended" (seaworthiness feature) is easy to conflate with "self-righting" (an unrelated capsize-recovery feature).

facts.topic: Construction / Lifeboat Shell — hull terminology (gunwale, stem, keel, stern post)
facts.fact: Lifeboats on modern ships are made of fiberglass; older lifeboats may be galvanized steel or aluminum.

facts.topic: Construction / Lifeboat Shell — hull terminology (gunwale, stem, keel, stern post)
facts.fact: Aluminum lifeboats are especially subject to electrolytic corrosion if iron or steel tools are left on or near the boat.

facts.topic: Construction / Lifeboat Shell — hull terminology (gunwale, stem, keel, stern post)
facts.fact: Only the Coast Guard can approve design/construction changes; emergency changes must be reported to the OCMI at the first port reached afterward.
facts.confusionNote: Who approves lifeboat design changes (only the Coast Guard) vs who emergency changes are reported to afterward (the OCMI) is easy to conflate.

facts.topic: Construction / Lifeboat Shell — hull terminology (gunwale, stem, keel, stern post)
facts.fact: Lifeboats are limited to 150-person capacity (based on 165 lbs average) and must be stopped, cleaned, and thoroughly overhauled at least once every year.

facts.topic: Construction / Lifeboat Shell — hull terminology (gunwale, stem, keel, stern post)
facts.fact: The keel, stem, and stern post together form the lifeboat's 'backbone'; the keel and gunwales give the boat lengthwise strength.

facts.topic: Construction / Lifeboat Shell — hull terminology (gunwale, stem, keel, stern post)
facts.fact: Hull plating rows top to bottom: sheer strake (just below gunwale), bilge strake (where sides turn toward bottom), garboard strake (bottom, next to keel).
facts.confusionNote: Top-to-bottom order of sheer/bilge/garboard strake is easy to mix up.

facts.topic: Hull plating terminology — sheer strake, bilge strake, garboard strake, doubler plating
facts.fact: The cradle holds the lifeboat in its stowed position; spots resting on the cradle need extra shell-plating thickness.

facts.topic: Hull plating terminology — sheer strake, bilge strake, garboard strake, doubler plating
facts.fact: This extra thickness is called 'doubler plating.'

facts.topic: Hull plating terminology — sheer strake, bilge strake, garboard strake, doubler plating
facts.fact: Doubler plating should be checked each time the boat is lifted or swung out.

facts.topic: Hull plating terminology — sheer strake, bilge strake, garboard strake, doubler plating
facts.fact: Sheer strake = top row of plating, just below the gunwale.
facts.confusionNote: Sheer strake (top, below gunwale) is easy to confuse with the other two strakes.

facts.topic: Hull plating terminology — sheer strake, bilge strake, garboard strake, doubler plating
facts.fact: Bilge strake = middle row, where the sides turn in toward the bottom of the lifeboat.
facts.confusionNote: Bilge strake (middle, where sides curve toward the bottom) is easy to confuse with garboard strake (bottom, next to keel).

facts.topic: Hull plating terminology — sheer strake, bilge strake, garboard strake, doubler plating
facts.fact: Garboard strake = bottom row, next to the keel.
facts.confusionNote: Garboard strake (bottom, next to keel) is easy to confuse with bilge strake.

facts.topic: Interior Construction — brackets, sidebench, thwarts, stanchions, buoyancy unit, grab rail, limber holes, keel, floors, bottom boards
facts.fact: Four sitting places in an open lifeboat: thwarts, side benches, bow sheets, stern sheets.
facts.confusionNote: Thwarts (athwartship seating for rowers) vs side benches (separate seating along the sides) are easy to conflate.

facts.topic: Interior Construction — brackets, sidebench, thwarts, stanchions, buoyancy unit, grab rail, limber holes, keel, floors, bottom boards
facts.fact: Thwarts extend side to side (athwartship), provide athwartship strength, and are where rowers sit while pulling oars.

facts.topic: Interior Construction — brackets, sidebench, thwarts, stanchions, buoyancy unit, grab rail, limber holes, keel, floors, bottom boards
facts.fact: Upright stanchions beneath each thwart give additional support to the thwart and strength to the boat.
facts.confusionNote: Stanchions (structural support) vs footrests/stretchers (feet-bracing) are both fittings near the thwarts but serve different purposes.

facts.topic: Interior Construction — brackets, sidebench, thwarts, stanchions, buoyancy unit, grab rail, limber holes, keel, floors, bottom boards
facts.fact: Footrests (stretchers) are provided for each thwart so rowers can brace their feet while rowing.
facts.confusionNote: Footrests/stretchers vs stanchions are easy to conflate.

facts.topic: Interior Construction — brackets, sidebench, thwarts, stanchions, buoyancy unit, grab rail, limber holes, keel, floors, bottom boards
facts.fact: Labeled interior parts include: gunwale, brackets, sidebench, stanchions, buoyancy unit, grab rail, limber holes, keel, floors, bottom boards (footings).

facts.topic: Interior parts continued — mast hasp, releasing hook/gear, bottom boards, thwarts, sidebenches, painter ring, bow/stern sheets
facts.fact: Brackets are metal bars supporting side benches and thwarts, running from gunwales to side benches, bolted through side benches and the ends of thwarts.

facts.topic: Interior parts continued — mast hasp, releasing hook/gear, bottom boards, thwarts, sidebenches, painter ring, bow/stern sheets
facts.fact: Bow sheets = seating area furthest forward; stern sheets = furthest aft seating area. The coxswain usually stands at the stern sheets while steering.
facts.confusionNote: Bow sheets (forward) and stern sheets (aft) seating locations are easy to swap.

facts.topic: Interior parts continued — mast hasp, releasing hook/gear, bottom boards, thwarts, sidebenches, painter ring, bow/stern sheets
facts.fact: Bottom boards (footing) are inside the bottom of the lifeboat, attached to and resting on floors (metal ribs running athwartship in the bilge).
facts.confusionNote: Floors (metal ribs, run athwartship) vs bottom boards (wooden, run lengthwise) are easy to mix up.

facts.topic: Interior parts continued — mast hasp, releasing hook/gear, bottom boards, thwarts, sidebenches, painter ring, bow/stern sheets
facts.fact: Bottom boards are wooden, run lengthwise, spread weight over the thin shell plating, add strength, and may be removed for painting/bottom maintenance.

facts.topic: Interior parts continued — mast hasp, releasing hook/gear, bottom boards, thwarts, sidebenches, painter ring, bow/stern sheets
facts.fact: A releasing gear ensures both ends of the lifeboat release from the falls at the same instant (full explanation given later, in the davits section).
facts.confusionNote: Students may assume the releasing gear relates to steering/launch speed rather than ensuring simultaneous release from both falls.

facts.topic: Buoyancy and Stability Features; Automatic Lifeboat Drain (drain cage/ball check)
facts.fact: Buoyancy units under each side bench keep a fully loaded lifeboat afloat even when full of water, and make the boat less likely to capsize.

facts.topic: Buoyancy and Stability Features; Automatic Lifeboat Drain (drain cage/ball check)
facts.fact: Buoyancy units are usually air tanks or unicellular plastic foam; removable for maintenance and must be re-stowed in their original position.

facts.topic: Buoyancy and Stability Features; Automatic Lifeboat Drain (drain cage/ball check)
facts.fact: An air-tank buoyancy unit can be checked for leaks: on a hot day, if the flat sides aren't bulging, it's probably leaking.

facts.topic: Buoyancy and Stability Features; Automatic Lifeboat Drain (drain cage/ball check)
facts.fact: Two or more drains are provided in each lifeboat (except free-fall lifeboats) to let water drain when the boat is lifted from the water.

facts.topic: Buoyancy and Stability Features; Automatic Lifeboat Drain (drain cage/ball check)
facts.fact: The automatic lifeboat drain uses a metal cage with a rubber ball that floats up and acts as an automatic closing valve.

facts.topic: Buoyancy and Stability Features; Automatic Lifeboat Drain (drain cage/ball check)
facts.fact: The first and most important thing to do when entering a lifeboat to prepare for launch is to close the drain by screwing on the metal cap.
facts.confusionNote: Students may assume the drain should be OPEN before launch -- closing it (screwing on the metal cap) is actually the first and most important launch-prep step.

facts.topic: Buoyancy and Stability Features; Automatic Lifeboat Drain (drain cage/ball check)
facts.fact: Open lifeboat drains should be open when stowed; enclosed boats may leave the cap on when stowed; free-fall lifeboats likely have no drain fitted.
facts.confusionNote: The open/enclosed/free-fall drain rules differ by lifeboat type and are easy to conflate.

facts.topic: Buoyancy and Stability Features; Automatic Lifeboat Drain (drain cage/ball check)
facts.fact: Limber holes are openings in the floors that let bilge water flow between compartments without going through the outer shell.
facts.confusionNote: Limber holes (drainage between compartments) are easy to confuse with buoyancy units (flotation, under side benches).

facts.topic: Exterior Rescue Features — grab rail, lifeline, seine float, rudder/nozzle
facts.fact: Grab rails (hand-holds) are metal bars on the outside of the lifeboat near the turn of the bilges, provided on lifeboats that don't self-right automatically.
facts.confusionNote: Students may assume grab rails are on ALL lifeboats -- they're specifically for lifeboats that do NOT self-right automatically.

facts.topic: Exterior Rescue Features — grab rail, lifeline, seine float, rudder/nozzle
facts.fact: Grab rails are secured so that if broken off, the lifeboat's plating will not get holes in it.

facts.topic: Exterior Rescue Features — grab rail, lifeline, seine float, rudder/nozzle
facts.fact: Lifelines must be at least 3/8 inch manila line (typical material: polypropylene multifilament).

facts.topic: Exterior Rescue Features — grab rail, lifeline, seine float, rudder/nozzle
facts.fact: Lifelines are divided into bights (loops) of three feet or less, each with a seine float to hold the line up in the water (float may be omitted if the line is inherently buoyant).
facts.confusionNote: Students may assume a seine float is always required on a lifeline -- it may be omitted if the line itself is inherently buoyant.

facts.topic: Exterior Rescue Features — grab rail, lifeline, seine float, rudder/nozzle
facts.fact: Lifelines serve two purposes: something for swimmers to hang onto, and something for people who can't find space in a crowded boat.

facts.topic: Steering Equipment — tiller, rudder, pintle, gudgeon, Kort Nozzle, steering wheel
facts.fact: Open lifeboats are typically fitted with an unbalanced rudder, controlled by a tiller attached to the top of the rudder, held in place by a toggle pin.

facts.topic: Steering Equipment — tiller, rudder, pintle, gudgeon, Kort Nozzle, steering wheel
facts.fact: The rudder is held in place by pintles and gudgeons (metal straps) on the stern post.
facts.confusionNote: Pintles and gudgeons (hold the rudder to the stern post) are easy to confuse with the toggle pin (holds the tiller to the rudder).

facts.topic: Steering Equipment — tiller, rudder, pintle, gudgeon, Kort Nozzle, steering wheel
facts.fact: Rudders are usually made of wood; sometimes watertight metal rudders that will float are used.

facts.topic: Steering Equipment — tiller, rudder, pintle, gudgeon, Kort Nozzle, steering wheel
facts.fact: Tiller-to-starboard moves the rudder to port, turning the boat to port; tiller-to-port moves the rudder to starboard, turning the boat to starboard.
facts.confusionNote: Classic inversion trap: tiller-to-starboard actually turns the boat to PORT, not starboard.

facts.topic: Steering Equipment — tiller, rudder, pintle, gudgeon, Kort Nozzle, steering wheel
facts.fact: In heavy seas, allow the bow to swing back and forth on each side of the desired heading — a steady course cannot be maintained in rough seas.

facts.topic: Steering Equipment — tiller, rudder, pintle, gudgeon, Kort Nozzle, steering wheel
facts.fact: Modern totally enclosed lifeboats commonly use rudder nozzles (Kort Nozzle), which also act as the required propeller guard and prevent fouling by debris; they improve directional control as thrust increases.
facts.confusionNote: Students may assume Kort Nozzles are purely a steering aid -- they also serve as the required propeller guard.

facts.topic: Steering Equipment — tiller, rudder, pintle, gudgeon, Kort Nozzle, steering wheel
facts.fact: Steering is normally by wheel; an emergency steering tiller is provided in case the wheel steering system fails.

facts.topic: Propulsion Systems / Engine Powered Systems — stern gland, stern tube, propeller shaft, engine
facts.fact: No engine will be used for any lifeboat if its fuel has a flashpoint of 43C or less.

facts.topic: Propulsion Systems / Engine Powered Systems — stern gland, stern tube, propeller shaft, engine
facts.fact: Diesel (compression-ignition) engines are preferred over gas because the fuel is less volatile; they have no spark plugs and work off the heat of compression.

facts.topic: Propulsion Systems / Engine Powered Systems — stern gland, stern tube, propeller shaft, engine
facts.fact: The propeller shaft passes through the hull via the stern tube; the stern gland is a packed ring inside the stern tube that stops water from entering the boat.
facts.confusionNote: Stern tube (the hollow pipe encasing the shaft) is easy to confuse with the stern gland (the packed ring inside it that seals against water).

facts.topic: Propulsion Systems / Engine Powered Systems — stern gland, stern tube, propeller shaft, engine
facts.fact: The quickest way to stop a diesel engine is to shut off the air (pull the stop lever out).

facts.topic: Propulsion Systems / Engine Powered Systems — stern gland, stern tube, propeller shaft, engine
facts.fact: Water-cooled engine lifeboats have two hull holes: one draws in seawater to cool the engine, the other discharges water with exhaust gases; some engines are air-cooled instead.

facts.topic: Propulsion Systems / Engine Powered Systems — stern gland, stern tube, propeller shaft, engine
facts.fact: A Class 1 motor lifeboat must start in all conditions, carry fuel for 24 hours continuous operation, and reach at least 6 knots in smooth water fully loaded.

facts.topic: Propulsion Systems / Engine Powered Systems — stern gland, stern tube, propeller shaft, engine
facts.fact: Lifeboats designed for more than 100 persons must be motor-propelled, except as a replacement for a previously approved hand-propelled lifeboat of that size.
facts.confusionNote: Students may assume ALL lifeboats over 100-person capacity must always be motor-propelled with no exceptions.

facts.topic: Engine starting/stopping procedures (manual/electric/hydraulic starting, electric-start checklist); propeller-characteristics intro
facts.fact: Lifeboat engines can run in any position during capsizing, and either continue running after the boat uprights, or stop during capsizing and can be easily restarted.
facts.confusionNote: Students may assume the engine must always keep running after a capsize -- it may either continue running OR stop and be restarted.

facts.topic: Engine starting/stopping procedures (manual/electric/hydraulic starting, electric-start checklist); propeller-characteristics intro
facts.fact: Fuel/lubrication system design prevents loss of more than 250 mL of lubricating oil from the engine during capsizing.

facts.topic: Engine starting/stopping procedures (manual/electric/hydraulic starting, electric-start checklist); propeller-characteristics intro
facts.fact: Enclosed and free-fall lifeboats are constructed to protect against harmful accelerations from launch or water impact.

facts.topic: Engine starting/stopping procedures (manual/electric/hydraulic starting, electric-start checklist); propeller-characteristics intro
facts.fact: The engine must be operated every abandon-ship drill; if not put in the water, run time should be limited (it will overheat); capable of operating at least 3 minutes after a cold start when the lifeboat is out of the water.

facts.topic: Engine starting/stopping procedures (manual/electric/hydraulic starting, electric-start checklist); propeller-characteristics intro
facts.fact: Three common engine-starting methods: manually (hand cranking), electric (battery-powered starting motor), hydraulic (stored hydraulic pressure).

facts.topic: Engine starting/stopping procedures (manual/electric/hydraulic starting, electric-start checklist); propeller-characteristics intro
facts.fact: Electric starting sequence: batteries ON, console power ON, transmission NEUTRAL, ignition ON, (if equipped) 'glow' for 5 seconds, ignition to 'START' and release once started.
facts.confusionNote: Order of the electric-start sequence steps is easy to mix up (e.g. assuming 'glow' comes before ignition ON).

facts.topic: Engine starting/stopping procedures (manual/electric/hydraulic starting, electric-start checklist); propeller-characteristics intro
facts.fact: A STOP switch/button is typically provided to stop the engine; older engines use a pull cable.

facts.topic: Engine starting/stopping procedures (manual/electric/hydraulic starting, electric-start checklist); propeller-characteristics intro
facts.fact: Propellers work efficiently producing thrust astern while propelling forward; when reversing, they operate inefficiently and create side thrust known as 'paddlewheel effect'.
facts.confusionNote: Students may assume 'paddlewheel effect' happens during forward propulsion -- it specifically occurs when the propeller is reversing.

facts.topic: Propeller rotation (right-hand propeller) and 'Safety Notes on Engine Powered Lifeboats' — fire extinguisher use steps
facts.fact: Propeller handedness is determined looking from astern, out of the water: clockwise rotation in forward gear = right-handed; counterclockwise = left-handed.

facts.topic: Propeller rotation (right-hand propeller) and 'Safety Notes on Engine Powered Lifeboats' — fire extinguisher use steps
facts.fact: Propeller side-thrust effect is greater going astern than going ahead.

facts.topic: Propeller rotation (right-hand propeller) and 'Safety Notes on Engine Powered Lifeboats' — fire extinguisher use steps
facts.fact: Right-handed propeller: going astern kicks the stern to port. Left-handed propeller: going astern kicks the stern to starboard.
facts.confusionNote: Right-handed vs left-handed propeller kick direction (port vs starboard) when backing is easy to swap.

facts.topic: Propeller rotation (right-hand propeller) and 'Safety Notes on Engine Powered Lifeboats' — fire extinguisher use steps
facts.fact: Most lifeboats have right-handed propellers, so backing with rudder amidships kicks the stern to port; right rudder must be applied to counteract this.

facts.topic: Propeller rotation (right-hand propeller) and 'Safety Notes on Engine Powered Lifeboats' — fire extinguisher use steps
facts.fact: Rudder nozzles (Kort Nozzles) minimize this unwanted stern movement.

facts.topic: Propeller rotation (right-hand propeller) and 'Safety Notes on Engine Powered Lifeboats' — fire extinguisher use steps
facts.fact: Fire extinguishers are stowed at each end of engine-powered lifeboats, rated for class B (diesel/flammable liquids) and class C (electrical) fires.
facts.confusionNote: Students may assume lifeboat extinguishers cover class A fires -- the manual specifies class B and class C only.

facts.topic: Propeller rotation (right-hand propeller) and 'Safety Notes on Engine Powered Lifeboats' — fire extinguisher use steps
facts.fact: ABC Dry-Chemical extinguisher steps: remove from bracket, hold upright, pull pin, squeeze lever handle, aim just under the flames, sweep side-to-side across the entire width of the fire.
facts.confusionNote: Students may assume you should aim directly at the visible flames rather than 'just under the flames'.

facts.topic: Hand Propelled Systems (Fleming gear) and hand/oar-propelled lifeboat rules
facts.fact: Fleming gear is a hand-propelling gear used in some open lifeboats (not used in new construction); includes a propeller, stern tube, stern gland, and gears.
facts.confusionNote: Students may assume Fleming gear is still used in new lifeboat construction -- the manual states it is not.

facts.topic: Hand Propelled Systems (Fleming gear) and hand/oar-propelled lifeboat rules
facts.fact: The boat is propelled by pulling lever handles fore and aft; sockets connect to bars that move cranks on the gear box, rotating the propeller shaft.

facts.topic: Hand Propelled Systems (Fleming gear) and hand/oar-propelled lifeboat rules
facts.fact: The gear box, near the stern, controls forward/reverse gears; water must not collect around it, since it could freeze in cold weather and jam the gears.

facts.topic: Hand Propelled Systems (Fleming gear) and hand/oar-propelled lifeboat rules
facts.fact: Oar-propelled lifeboat capacity is limited by regulation to a maximum of 59 persons.
facts.confusionNote: The 59-person oar-propelled cap is easy to confuse with the adjacent 60-100 and >100 regulatory bands.

facts.topic: Hand Propelled Systems (Fleming gear) and hand/oar-propelled lifeboat rules
facts.fact: Lifeboats for 60-100 persons must be hand-propelled (Fleming gear) or motor-propelled; over 100 persons must be motor-propelled.
facts.confusionNote: The 59-person oar-propelled cap is easy to confuse with the adjacent 60-100 and >100 regulatory bands.

facts.topic: Hand Propelled Systems (Fleming gear) and hand/oar-propelled lifeboat rules
facts.fact: All open lifeboats (even motorized or Fleming-gear ones) must carry at least 4 rowing oars and one steering oar (sweep).

facts.topic: Hand Propelled Systems (Fleming gear) and hand/oar-propelled lifeboat rules
facts.fact: Free-fall davit-launched enclosed lifeboats may carry no oars, since oars risk injuring personnel at launch and the boat's launch inertia moves it away from the ship by design.
facts.confusionNote: Students may assume all lifeboats must carry oars -- free-fall davit-launched enclosed lifeboats are a specific exception.
